title: On small sums of roots of unity
speaker: Philipp Habegger
abstract: Let $k$ be a fixed positive integer. Myerson (and others) asked how small the modulus of a non-zero sum of $k$ roots of unity can be. If the roots of unity have order dividing $N$, then an elementary argument shows that the modulus decreases at most exponentially in $N$ (for fixed $k$). Moreover it is known that the decay is at worst polynomial if $k < 5$. But no general sub-exponential bound is known if $k \geq 5$.

proof_stages.label: Myerson small-sum problem and polynomial conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the problem of lower-bounding nonzero sums of a fixed number of roots of unity, recalls the trivial algebraic-norm exponential bound and the easy low-term geometric cases, and formulates the folklore expectation of polynomial decay.

proof_stages.label: Prime-order sparsity theorem for small conjugates
proof_stages.summary: The first main theorem is stated as evidence for the conjecture: for p-th roots of unity, within a single Galois orbit only p^epsilon conjugates of a nonzero fixed-length sum can be exceptionally small.

proof_stages.label: O-minimal approximation proof
proof_stages.summary: Roots of unity are encoded as rational points near the definable zero locus of an exponential sum, Łojasiewicz inequalities convert small values into closeness to that locus, and an approximate Pila-Wilkie counting theorem yields the sparsity result, with the prime-order hypothesis used to control semi-algebraic exceptional curves.

proof_stages.label: Gaussian periods as all-conjugates small sums
proof_stages.summary: To make the Galois action useful, the second part replaces a single small sum by the whole orbit average encoded by Gaussian periods attached to subgroups of F_p^*.

proof_stages.label: Mahler-measure limit mechanism
proof_stages.summary: Using the n=3 Gaussian-period case as the model, the normalized logarithmic norm is rewritten by a resultant identity in terms of roots of a univariate polynomial, whose limiting contribution is a Mahler measure via Lawton's theorem provided the error is controlled.

proof_stages.label: Error terms reduced to near-vanishing root-of-unity sums
proof_stages.summary: The difficult error arises only when a root of the auxiliary polynomial lies near the unit circle and its p-th power is near 1, which is translated back into the need to control roots of unity that almost vanish in the original fixed-length sum.

proof_stages.label: Odd-prime Gaussian-period theorem
proof_stages.summary: For an odd prime number of terms n and primes p congruent to 1 modulo n, the orbit average of the logarithm of the Gaussian period converges to the Mahler measure of x_1+...+x_n with a polynomial error, using the sparsity theorem for most conjugates and Mahler-type root-separation/transcendence input for the exceptional ones.

proof_stages.label: Comparison with known finite-subgroup limit results
proof_stages.summary: The final discussion situates the result among qualitative ergodic and transcendence-theoretic work of Lind, Schmidt, Verbitsky, and Schmidt on polynomial evaluations over finite subgroups, emphasizing differences in hypotheses and error terms.

examples.name: One- through four-term geometric sums
examples.note: The cases of one, two, three, and four roots of unity are described via elementary geometry, especially almost-triangles and almost-rhombi, to illustrate why polynomial lower bounds are easy before the five-term threshold.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the 1-, 2-, 3-, and 4-term cases geometrically, deriving the orders 1, 1/N, and 1/N^2 via almost-triangles and almost-rhombi

passes.reason: These are solved low-term base/boundary cases: one term is trivial and the four-term rhombus case is the tight last elementary case before five terms.
The one-to-four-term sums are low-term base cases, with the four-term almost-rhombus giving the tight geometric behavior rather than refuting a claim.

examples.name: Periodic zero set versus a fundamental domain
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains that the full periodic zero set is not accessible to o-minimality, illustrating why the argument restricts to the unit cube or fundamental domain.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs the zero set, relates nearby rational points to it, and checks why one must restrict to the [0,1] fundamental domain for o-minimal definability rather than use the full periodic set

passes.reason: The full zero set has translation-periodic regularity and is cited as the obstruction to global o-minimal definability, forcing restriction to a fundamental domain.

examples.name: Even-term boundary cases
examples.note: The contrast between the special four-term geometric case and the open even cases such as six terms illustrates the present theorem's restriction to odd orders and the limitation of the method.
